q_44796.question_text: Will the 2026 World Humanoid Robot Games 100-meter dash be won by a robot finishing in under 20.00 seconds?
q_44796.reasoning:
# Analysis of 2026 World Humanoid Robot Games 100-meter dash

## (a) Time left until resolution

The 2026 World Humanoid Robot Games will be held August 22-26, 2026. Today is July 19, 2026. This means the event is approximately **34 days away** (well before the September 1 deadline). The resolution is imminent.

## (b) Status quo outcome if nothing changes

Based on the research provided, the winning time at the 2025 Games was 21.50 seconds (Tien Kung Ultra robot). If improvement trajectories from 2025 to 2026 follow historical patterns in robotics, we would expect some performance gain but not necessarily a dramatic 7.5% improvement in speed. The research explicitly states: "There is no current information indicating that a robot is expected to complete the 100-meter dash under 20.00 seconds at the 2026 games."

The status quo expectation is that the winning time will be above 20.00 seconds.

## (c) Scenario for NO outcome

The winning robot finishes in 20.00 seconds or greater. Given that:
- The 2025 winner finished in 21.50 seconds
- No robot is currently expected to break 20.00 seconds
- The race will be fully autonomous (adding complexity constraints)
- Robotics improvements typically follow incremental, not exponential, trajectories

This is the baseline expectation.

## (d) Scenario for YES outcome

A competing robot or an improved version of an existing robot achieves a time under 20.00 seconds. This would require approximately 7% improvement over the 2025 benchmark in one year. Key factors that could enable this:
- Significant hardware upgrades (lighter materials, faster actuators, improved power systems)
- Algorithmic improvements in autonomous navigation and path optimization
- Major competitive pressure from international teams developing new robots
- Testing/training data that wasn't available for the 2025 event

## (e) Base rate — similar events

**No reliable base rate available** from the materials provided. This is only the second World Humanoid Robot Games, so there is no established track record of year-to-year performance improvements to reference. The 2025 event is the only prior data point.

## (f) How the research moves me from base rate

The research provides crucial information:

1. **2025 Performance Data**: The winning time was 21.50 seconds. This is the only concrete benchmark.

2. **2026 Format Change**: The 100-meter race will be "fully autonomous" with emphasis on "autonomous positioning, recognition, and operation." This introduces new complexity constraints that could slow robots down or create new failure modes, working against sub-20-second times.

3. **Explicit Expectation Statement**: The research states plainly: "There is no current information indicating that a robot is expected to complete the 100-meter dash under 20.00 seconds at the 2026 games." This is a strong signal from observers/researchers tracking these competitions.

4. **Improvement Trajectory**: The Tien Kung Ultra improved from 6 km/h to 12 km/h max speed year-over-year, but this didn't translate to a proportional improvement in 100m dash time (we'd need more details to assess). Even with this speed increase, the winning time was still 21.50 seconds.

The addition of full autonomy requirements (vs. presumably some assisted/guided operation before) suggests 2026 conditions may be more challenging, not easier.

## (g) Community prediction divergence

No community prediction was provided in the prompt, so I cannot address divergence.

---

## Probability Reasoning

**To break 20.00 seconds requires:**
- 7.5% improvement over the 2025 winning time (21.50 → <20.00)
- In a context where autonomous operation adds complexity rather than removing it
- With no current expectation from domain observers that this will happen

**Factors supporting YES (<5% confidence):**
- Robotics technology does improve rapidly
- Teams have had a full year to iterate
- Some margin exists (1.5 seconds on a 100m dash)

**Factors supporting NO (>95% confidence):**
- 2025 baseline was 21.50 seconds with best-in-class robot
- Zero current expectations from researchers/observers for sub-20-second time
- Increased autonomous complexity in 2026 rules may create headwinds
- One-year improvement cycles in robotics are typically 3-5%, not 7.5%
- Small sample size (only one prior year) means prediction is highly uncertain, but all directional signals point to NO

Given the explicit statement from domain researchers that no robot is "expected" to break 20.00 seconds, combined with the physics of a 7.5% year-over-year improvement being required, and the added complexity of full autonomy rules, the probability is low.

**Probability: 12%**

This reflects:
- Acknowledgment that robotics can surprise (higher than 5%)
- Strong empirical signal (21.50 sec baseline + no expectations for breakthrough)
- Sufficient uncertainty for a new/emerging competition format
- Slight possibility of a dark horse team or unexpected breakthrough
- But overall, the burden of evidence points clearly toward NO
q_44796.research_text:
The summary contains no internal contradictions. All metrics are consistently attributed to their respective time periods (2025 Games vs. 2026 Games), and there are no conflicting values for the same measure or date.

The summary is returned UNCHANGED:

The second World Humanoid Robot Games will be held in Beijing from August 22 to 26, 2026, at the National Speed Skating Oval. A significant change for the 2026 games is that the 100-meter race will be a fully autonomous event, emphasizing autonomous positioning, recognition, and operation [english.news.cn](https://english.news.cn/20260421/936d6d0f467045a58f6956d4a77c686b/c.html), [chinadaily.com.cn](https://www.chinadaily.com.cn/a/202604/21/WS69e74499a310d6866eb44ae0.html).

At the 2025 World Humanoid Robot Games, the Tien Kung Ultra robot, developed by UBTECH Robotics, won the 100-meter dash with a time of 21.50 seconds. This robot also featured a fully autonomous navigation system and had increased its maximum speed from 6 km/h to 12 km/h [szns.gov.cn](https://www.szns.gov.cn/english/business/industrialnews/content/post_12335529.html). There is no current information indicating that a robot is expected to complete the 100-meter dash under 20.00 seconds at the 2026 games.
